metadata.title: A Robust Test for the Stationarity Assumption in Sequential Decision Making

sections.title: Preliminary
content: Sequential Decision Making
Markov Decision Process (MDP): $p_t(S_{t+1}, R_t | A_t, S_t)$.
Stationarity assumption: $H_0: \{p_j(S_{j+1}, R_j | S_j, A_j)\}_{0 \le j \le T-1}$ are homogeneous over time $j$;
Offline reinforcement learning (RL): static dataset without further interactions.
Additional challenges in modern RL problems:
State variable $S_t$ can be high-dimensional (e.g., 7056 for Atari).
Dynamics $p_t$ can be highly complex (e.g., autonomous driving).
Modern ML methods well-suited for non-linearity and high-dimensionality, however, need careful treatment for proper statistical inference.

sections.title: Proposed Test
content: Graphical illustration
The proposed test is based on sample splitting[1].

sections.title: Maximum-type test statistic
content: $$\widehat{\Gamma} = \max_{\substack{\epsilon T \le t \le (1-\epsilon)T \\ b \in \{1, \dots, B\}}} \frac{t(T-t)}{T^2} \widehat{\sigma}(t, h_b)^{-1} \frac{1}{|I_2|} \sum_{i=1}^{|I_2|} \widehat{\psi}_i,$$
$h_b$ is a test function from a rich function class and $B$ is the number of test functions; $g$ is some reference density function. $\epsilon \in (0, 0.5)$ is the boundary removal parameter.
$\sqrt{t(T-t)/T^2} \widehat{\sigma}(t, h_b)^{-1}$ is the normalized factor for different $(t, h_b)$ where $\widehat{\sigma}^2(t, h_b)$ is the sampling variance estimator.

Nuisance functions $\widehat{p}_{[0,t]}, \widehat{p}_{[t,T]}, \widehat{\omega}_{[0,t]}, \widehat{\omega}_{[t,T]}$ are estimated using the data from $I_1$. One can use flexible machine/deep learning tools. The estimator $\widehat{\psi}_i$ is evaluated by directly plugging estimated nuisance functions and samples from $I_2$.

Gaussian multiplier bootstrap is used to approximate the limiting distribution and compute the resulting p-value, i.e., $\widehat{p} = \sum_{q=1}^Q I(\widehat{\Gamma}^{(q)} \ge \widehat{\Gamma}) / Q$.

sections.title: Theoretical Property
content: Double Robustness: Suppose $H_0$ holds. For any $h \in H$ and $t \in (0, T-1)$, we have $E \psi_i^* = 0, i = 1, \dots, N,$

Moreover, the above equation is doubly robust, i.e., for any $p_{[0,t]}, p_{[t,T]}, \omega_{[0,t]}, \omega_{[t,T]}$, the following holds as long as either $p_{[0,t]} = p^*$ or $\omega_{[0,t]} = \omega_{[0,t]}^*, \omega_{[t,T]} = \omega_{[t,T]}^*$, $E \psi_i = 0, i = 1, \dots, N.$

$\psi_i^*$ is the oracle version of $\widehat{\psi}_i$ by replacing the estimated nuisance functions with the oracle ones. $\psi_i$ is similar to $\widehat{\psi}_i$ by replacing estimated ones with general functions.

Size: Suppose $B = O((NT)^c)$ for any $c > 0$ and $t = O(T)$ for any $t \in [\epsilon T, (1-\epsilon)T]$ hold. In addition, suppose uniform boundness of $\frac{g}{\omega_{[0,t]}}, \frac{g}{\omega_{[t,T]}}$, strict stationarity and geometrical ergodicity of $\{S_t\}_{t \ge 0}$ as $T \to \infty$ and the convergence speed of $\widehat{p}_{[0,t]}, \widehat{p}_{[t,T]}, \widehat{\omega}_{[0,t]}, \widehat{\omega}_{[t,T]}$ to oracle value satisfying
$\kappa_1, \kappa_2, \kappa_3, \kappa_4 \in (0, 1/2)$ and $\kappa_1 + \kappa_3 > \frac{1}{2}, \kappa_2 + \kappa_4 > \frac{1}{2},$
we have $P(\widehat{p} \ge \alpha | H_0) = \alpha + o(1)$

content: Fig. A: Double Robustness.
Fig. B: Applicability to online settings.
Fig. C: Superiority under high-dimensional settings

sections.title: Application to Intern Health Study Data
content: Intern Health Study [2]: Wearable and survey data of 1565 medical interns in the U.S. collected for 26 weeks (from July 1, 2018, to Dec 31, 2018).
The goal is to improve the health-related outcome of medical interns through mobile-based push notification.
Every week, each intern would be either randomly assigned to notification week ($A_t = 1$) with 0.75 probability or non-notification week ($A_t = 0$).
Intern's previous week's average daily step count, sleep duration and survey mood score are considered as the state $S_t$.
We consider the next week daily average step count as the reward $R_t$.
The treatment effect of push notification decrease over time.

sections.title: References
content: [1] V. Chernozhukov et al., "Double/debiased machine learning for treatment and structural parameters," The Econometrics Journal, vol. 21, no. 1, pp. C1-C68.

[2] T. NeCamp et al., "Assessing real-time moderation for developing adaptive mobile health interventions for medical interns: Micro-randomized trial," Journal of Medical Internet Research, vol. 22, no. 3, 2020.
